items.title: Variance as scatter across training sets
items.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  pop["Same data population"]
  d1["Training set D1"]
  d2["Training set D2"]
  d3["Training set D3"]
  p1["Prediction curve 1"]
  p2["Prediction curve 2"]
  p3["Prediction curve 3"]
  mean["Mean prediction E_D[y hat]"]
  pop --> d1
  pop --> d2
  pop --> d3
  d1 --> p1
  d2 --> p2
  d3 --> p3
  p1 --> mean
  p2 --> mean
  p3 --> mean
items.caption: Different training samples produce different fitted models; variance measures how far those predictions spread around their average at each input x.

items.title: Bias-variance-noise decomposition
items.mermaid:
flowchart TB
  err["Expected squared error E[(y - y hat)^2]"]
  err --> bias2["Bias squared: systematic error"]
  err --> var["Variance: instability across training sets"]
  err --> noise["Irreducible noise Var(epsilon|x)"]
items.caption: Total prediction error splits into bias² (wrong average prediction), variance (sensitivity to training sample), and noise you cannot model away.

items.title: Complexity drives variance up
items.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  simple["Simple model"]
  complex["Flexible model"]
  simple --> lowv["Lower variance, often higher bias"]
  complex --> highv["Higher variance, often lower bias"]
items.caption: Increasing model capacity typically reduces bias but increases variance on finite data—the core tension of the bias–variance tradeoff.
